---
title: "Minimum Track Record Length Under Fat Tails"
subtitle: "How Long Must Performance History Be Before Skill Is Declared?"
author: "Jean-Marc Choufani"
date: "2026"
bibliography: ../references.bib
---

> **Status:** Planned — research literature surveyed, writing not yet begun

---

::: {.callout-note}
## Chapter forthcoming
:::

## Motivation

@bailey2012 derive the Minimum Track Record Length (MinTRL) — the number of observations
needed to reject, at a given confidence level, the null hypothesis that a manager's Sharpe
ratio is zero. The derivation assumes finite fourth moment ($\kappa > 4$).

@kao2025 proved in 2025 that the Sharpe ratio converges at rate $n^{1-2/\kappa}$ when
$\kappa \in (2, 4)$ — slower than the $\sqrt{T}$ Gaussian rate — toward a stable
distribution with tail index $\kappa/2$.

The practical table that follows from these results does not exist: "At tail exponent
$\hat{\alpha}$ and significance level $p$, the minimum track record is $N$ years."

## The Claim

At $\hat{\alpha} = 2.69$ (the median in our equity sample), the minimum track record
required to declare skill at 95% confidence is substantially longer than Gaussian theory
implies. The precise factor is derivable analytically from the @kao2025 convergence rate
and verifiable against our cross-sectional $\hat{\alpha}$ distribution.

The implication is philosophical as much as practical: for a large fraction of the
equity universe, the sample sizes available to any practitioner are insufficient to
distinguish skill from luck at conventional significance levels — not because the
practitioner is unlucky, but because the distribution does not permit it.

## Key prior work

- @bailey2012 — Probabilistic Sharpe Ratio and MinTRL
- @bailey2014 — Deflated Sharpe Ratio
- @kao2025 — convergence rate under fat tails (critical new anchor)
- @vlasiuk2025 — Lévy-stable scaling of performance metrics
- Gap: no explicit $N(\hat{\alpha}, \text{significance})$ table. **Novelty: HIGH.**
